content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 16, 2016

Bruce Gibson
Supervisor District 2
San Luis Obispo County
1055 Monterey, Room D430
San Luis Obispo, CA 93408-1003

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-012

Dear Mr. Gibson:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Under the Act, may you participate in decisions before your county's Local Agency Formation Commission ("LAFCO") to dissolve the Cayucos Fire Protection District ("CFPD") when you own real property within the district?

2. If there is a conflict of interest, does the "public generally" exception apply?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. You have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act prohibiting you from participating in the potential LAFCO decision concerning the dissolution of the CFPD.

2. No. The "public generally" exception is not applicable.

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-012
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a San Luis Obispo County Supervisor representing District 2. You also serve as one
of two county supervisors appointed to the county's LAFCO. You own a single family home in the
community of Cayucos. The CFPD provides fire protection within Cayucos. Due to revenue
constraints and the difficulty of maintaining a volunteer firefighting staff, the CFPD board has
discussed the possibility of dissolving the district. The CFPD does not currently have a dissolution
application before the county LAFCO and it is unknown at this time whether they will submit an
application. If the CFPD dissolves, fire protection will be provided by SLO County Fire and/or
CALfire. The Cayucos Urban Reserve Line is essentially identical to the CFPD boundary and there
are 2,592 residents in the town.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(b).)

Based on the information you have provided, you have an interest in real property resulting
from your ownership of a single family home in the town of Cayucos.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A conflict of interest arises when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. The standard for foreseeability
differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly involved in the governmental decision.

Pursuant to Regulation 18701(a), an interest is directly involved in a governmental decision,
and presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it involves "any governmental decision affecting a
real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6)," which determines
whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on real property is material. Regulation
18702.2(a)(2) provides the financial effect on real property is material whenever the decision
"determines the parcel's inclusion or exclusion from any city, county, district…"

We have advised that a decision to form a LAFCO district, which would include an
official's real property interest, would be a decision that determined a parcel's inclusion in a
district. (Geyer Advice Letter, No. A-15-132.) Similarly, we find that a decision to dissolve a
LAFCO district would determine a parcel's exclusion from the district.

Should the CFPD submit an application for dissolution, the San Luis Obispo County
LAFCO would be required to either approve or deny the application. Because you own real
property within the CFPD boundary, your real property interest would be explicitly involved in the
decision pursuant to Regulation 18701(a). Specifically, the decision to dissolve the CFPD
necessarily excludes your real property pursuant to 18702.2(a)(2).

File No. A-16-012
Page No. 3

Accordingly, the LAFCO decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your real property. Therefore, the Act prohibits you from participating in the LAFCO decision to approve or deny the CFPD's potential application for dissolution.

Public Generally

Regulation 18703 states the financial effect is indistinguishable if "a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment." With respect to real property, a "significant segment of the public" consists of at least 25% of all real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(2).)

You have not provided specific facts about the number of real properties in the relevant jurisdictions. Your facts state, however, that the population within the Cayuco's Urban Reserve Line is 2,592. The estimated population of San Luis Obispo County, which is coextensive with LAFCO's jurisdiction, is 279,083 residents.²

Based on these facts, the 25% threshold would not appear to be satisfied. Accordingly, the "public generally" exception does not apply. Therefore, you may not participate in the LAFCO decision regarding whether or not the CFPD should be dissolved.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-0947.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Lauren Ngo
Intern, Legal Division

LN:jgl

² http://quickfacts.census.gov/qfd/states/06 06079.html
